retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate truncus arteriosus from other congenital cardiac defects based on clinical features. Select the appropriate diagnostic tests for a patient with possible truncus arteriosus. Apply evidence-based guidelines in formulating an individualized management plan for a patient with truncus arteriosus. Collaborate effectively within an interprofessional team to coordinate care, address complex medical issues, and support the holistic needs of patients with truncus arteriosus. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Truncus arteriosus is an uncommon cyanotic congenital cardiac defect characterized by a single large arterial trunk with a semilunar valve overriding a ventricular septal defect. Pulmonary vascular resistance determines pulmonary blood flow as pulmonary arteries arise directly from the truncus. Since mortality is high for truncus arteriosus, early intervention and surgical treatment are imperative, especially within the 1st year of life. With improved survival rates, comprehensive care for congenital heart disease must address transition challenges, including medical, psychosocial, and lifestyle considerations. Truncus arteriosus significantly affects mental health due to its impact on several organ systems and requirement for continuous medical care from a young age.

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The initial management involves stabilizing the patient and balancing blood flow through the pulmonary and systemic circuits. Interventions should maintain stable ductal patency in patients with interrupted aortic arch. Care is typically provided in a neonatal (NICU) or cardiac intensive care unit (CICU).

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Truncus arteriosus presents with a single arterial trunk originating from the heart, often with a VSD. Echocardiography is the best initial imaging modality, though cardiac catheterization and advanced imaging can give additional diagnostic insights. Surgery is the only definitive treatment for patients with truncus arteriosus requiring repeat interventions. Mortality is high within the 1st year of life without surgery. Patients usually still require multiple cardiac reinterventions postoperatively. Thus, long-term follow-up with a congenital heart disease specialist is advised. Genetic counseling informs families about associated genetic factors and assists in reproductive decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with truncus arteriosus often have a prenatal history that may include abnormal cardiac findings on fetal ultrasound or echocardiography. Additionally, there might be a family history of congenital heart disease or genetic syndromes associated with cardiac anomalies. Previous medical history may include cardiac interventions or surgeries. Developmental milestones should be carefully assessed, as some patients may have delays or neurodevelopmental abnormalities associated with the underlying condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Truncus arteriosus has an annual incidence of 7 per 100,000 live births. While the condition accounts for less than 1% of all congenital heart lesions, truncus arteriosus accounts for approximately 4% of critical congenital heart defects. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity for healthcare professionals is designed to enhance learners' competence in evaluating and managing truncus arteriosus. After participation, learners gain proficiency in recognizing the signs and symptoms of truncus arteriosus and determining individualized diagnostic and treatment approaches for patients with this condition. Participants learn to incorporate evidence-based management strategies to contribute effectively within an interprofessional team to optimize outcomes for individuals with truncus arteriosus.

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: New technological advances have improved the rate of diagnosing truncus arteriosus prenatally to greater than 90%. However, truncus arteriosus may be misdiagnosed as pulmonary atresia with a VSD if structures and aortopulmonary collaterals cannot be correctly identified on prenatal echocardiography. [32] [33] [34] Other differential diagnoses for a single outflow tract ventricle include a double outlet right ventricle with pulmonary and aortic atresia. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Introduction -- Classification
retrieved_docs.content: Collett and Edwards Type IV truncus arteriosus is currently considered a form of pulmonary atresia. This differentiation emphasizes the specific developmental irregularities in each condition, distinguishing between truncus arteriosus' characteristic septation abnormalities and pulmonary atresia's subpulmonary myocardial developmental anomalies. [12] [13] Meanwhile, the Van Praagh classification includes the following truncus arteriosus subtypes:

retrieved_docs.title: Patent Ductus Arteriosus -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery fistula Sinus of Valsalva aneurysm Aortopulmonary defect Persistent truncus arteriosus Pulmonary arteriovenous fistula Total anomalous pulmonary venous return

retrieved_docs.title: Partial and Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Connection -- Differential Diagnosis -- TAPVC
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses for TAPVC includes the following: Obstructed TAPVC Respiratory Distress Syndrome: Signs typically begin after birth, unlike TAPVC, where the presentation is slightly delayed (typically occurring after 12 hours of life). Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn 2. Unobstructed TAPVC: Large ASD, VSD Truncus arteriosus AV canal defects Single ventricle lesions

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Treatment / Management -- Immediate Postoperative Care
retrieved_docs.content: Both noninvasive and invasive methods are utilized for continuous monitoring in the CICU. While noninvasive monitoring includes basic parameters such as heart rate, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation, invasive modalities like pulmonary and atrial catheters and central and arterial lines provide detailed cardiopulmonary information. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: The severity of pulmonary blood flow variations, semilunar valvular abnormalities, and aortic arch obstruction drives the clinical picture. Birth history may reveal cyanosis or respiratory distress soon after delivery, prompting further evaluation. The critical congenital heart defect screen within the first few days after birth might show mild or moderate desaturation. Unscreened infants may present within the first 2 weeks with symptoms of congestive heart failure resulting from increased blood flow to the lungs as PVR naturally decreases. These infants may exhibit poor feeding, failure to thrive, lethargy, tachypnea, costosternal retractions, grunting, nasal flaring, tachycardia, hepatomegaly, and persistent cyanosis despite oxygen therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Postnatal diagnosis is ascertained with a transthoracic echocardiogram showing the lesion's precise anatomy and type. Occasionally, cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) or computed tomography angiography (CTA) is used to evaluate coronary artery anatomy and branch pulmonary arteries, especially in Van Praagh Type A3. This modality also helps determine whether aortic interruption or coarctation is present, which is pertinent to surgical planning. Postoperatively, CTA helps evaluate the pulmonary homograft and pulmonary artery anatomy for recurrent or residual stenosis. [26] Cardiac catheterization is mainly reserved for interventions, such as balloon angioplasty or stenting to dilate narrowed pulmonary arteries between surgical procedures. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Treatment / Management -- Prostaglandin Infusion
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandins promote ductal patency if a concurrent aortic arch anomaly exists. Maintaining ductal patency is crucial for ensuring adequate systemic blood flow.

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Truncus arteriosus is a rare, congenital, cyanotic cardiac anomaly characterized by a ventricular septal defect (VSD), a single truncal valve, and a common ventricular outflow tract. Systemic and pulmonary venous blood mix at the VSD and desaturated blood is ejected into the outflow tract. The pulmonary arteries arise directly from the truncus. Thus, pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) determines the amount of pulmonary blood flow (PBF). Death in infancy is probable without surgical intervention. Long-term surgical outcomes are positive, but residual and potential complications require long-term cardiology follow-up. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical association between اضطراب النظام (systemic disorder) and تدلي الصمام المترالي (mitral valve prolapse) indicates that patients who exhibit systemic dysregulation may have an increased likelihood of developing mitral valve prolapse, a condition in which the mitral valve leaflets bulge into the left atrium during systole. Recognizing this link helps clinicians consider valvular assessment in individuals with systemic disorders, facilitating earlier diagnosis and more comprehensive management of potential cardiac involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This condition involves abnormal dilation of blood vessels that is present from birth and is considered within the spectrum of diseases affecting heart valves. Recognizing congenital vascular dilatation as a valvular heart disease aids clinicians in diagnosing and monitoring potential valve dysfunction, altered hemodynamics, and related complications such as heart failure or arrhythmias, thereby guiding appropriate management and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1496
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) is clinically associated with valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي), indicating that patients with valvular lesions may present with this acute coronary syndrome. Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) occurs within the spectrum of valvular heart disease, reflecting abnormal displacement of the mitral leaflets that contributes to valve dysfunction. Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease, highlighting that certain vascular anomalies are considered part of the broader valvular disease category. Understanding how unstable angina, mitral valve prolapse, and congenital vascular dilatation relate to valvular heart disease helps clinicians assess and manage patients with complex cardiac pathology more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1724
retrieved_docs.content: Vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية) is a broad vascular condition that includes a specific subtype called congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري). Congenital vascular dilatation is classified as a form of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This hierarchical relationship indicates that the congenital form of vascular dilatation is both a child category of general vascular dilatation and a distinct type of valvular heart disease, highlighting its relevance in the diagnosis and management of cardiac valve disorders.
